name: metasploit-framework
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "use Metasploit for penetration testing", "exploit vulnerabilities with msfconsole", "create payloads with msfvenom", "perform post-exploitation", "use auxiliary modules for scanning", or "develop custom exploits". It provides comprehensive guidance for leveraging the Metasploit Framework in security assessments.
capabilities: Guide users through Metasploit Framework console operations and module selection
Explain exploit configuration, payload types, and target-specific setup procedures
Document Meterpreter post-exploitation commands for authorized security assessments
Provide msfvenom payload generation examples for various platforms and formats
Cover auxiliary modules for reconnaissance, scanning, and enumeration
Include troubleshooting guidance for common exploitation and session management issues
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill provides documentation for the Metasploit Framework, a legitimate penetration testing tool used by security professionals. All detected patterns are educational examples showing authorized security testing workflows. The skill includes appropriate legal disclaimers emphasizing written authorization requirements. While it documents offensive security capabilities that could be misused, the context clearly indicates professional security assessment purposes with proper legal safeguards.
valueStatement: Security professionals need expert guidance for authorized penetration testing workflows. This skill provides comprehensive Metasploit Framework documentation covering exploitation, payload generation, and post-exploitation activities with legal best practices.
userTitle: Master Metasploit Penetration Testing with Claude
limitations: This skill provides documentation and guidance only - does not execute Metasploit commands directly
Requires Metasploit Framework to be installed on the user's system separately
All penetration testing activities require written authorization and legal compliance
Exploit success depends on target vulnerabilities, network configuration, and security controls
Modern antivirus and EDR solutions may detect and block Metasploit payloads
Does not provide custom exploit development or vulnerability research services

useCases.title: Penetration Testing Workflow Guidance
useCases.description: Security professionals use this skill during authorized penetration testing engagements to navigate Metasploit Framework capabilities, select appropriate exploit modules, configure payloads, and conduct post-exploitation activities within legal scope.

useCases.targetUser: Red team operators and security auditors
useCases.title: Security Assessment Documentation
useCases.description: Red team members leverage this skill to document Metasploit usage in security assessment reports, ensuring proper command syntax, module selection, and post-exploitation activities are recorded accurately for client deliverables.

useCases.title: Educational Resource for Security Training
useCases.description: Cybersecurity students and certification candidates use this skill to learn Metasploit Framework concepts, practice penetration testing workflows in lab environments, and prepare for certifications like OSCP, CEH, and PTX.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Module Search and Selection
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I search for and select an appropriate Metasploit exploit module for a Windows target with SMB vulnerabilities?
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner starting with Metasploit Framework reconnaissance and exploit selection

promptTemplates.title: Exploit Configuration and Execution
promptTemplates.prompt: Guide me through configuring the eternalblue exploit with a meterpreter reverse_tcp payload, including setting RHOSTS, LHOST, and required options.
promptTemplates.scenario: Intermediate user setting up a specific exploit with proper parameters

promptTemplates.title: Post-Exploitation with Meterpreter
promptTemplates.prompt: After establishing a Meterpreter session, what commands should I use to enumerate the target system, harvest credentials, and maintain persistence during an authorized penetration test?
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced user conducting post-exploitation activities in a security assessment

promptTemplates.title: Payload Generation with msfvenom
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate an msfvenom command to create a Windows executable payload with reverse TCP connection, including encoding to evade antivirus detection.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced user creating custom payloads for authorized testing scenarios

bestPractices: Always obtain written authorization before conducting any penetration testing activities with Metasploit
Use the 'check' command first to verify target vulnerability before running exploits to minimize disruption
Test payloads in isolated lab environments before deploying to production assessment targets
Document all commands, results, and findings for inclusion in penetration test reports
Use encrypted reverse_https payloads instead of reverse_tcp when possible to evade network monitoring
Background sessions and migrate processes immediately to maintain access stability
antiPatterns: Do not use Metasploit exploits on systems without explicit written authorization - this is illegal
Avoid running all exploits sequentially against targets without proper reconnaissance and vulnerability verification
Never use production systems or public IP addresses in example commands during documentation or training
Do not leave active Meterpreter sessions unattended or forget to properly close them after assessments
Avoid using unencoded payloads that are easily detected by antivirus without testing evasion techniques first

faq.question: Is Metasploit Framework legal to use?
faq.answer: Yes, Metasploit is a legitimate penetration testing tool used by security professionals worldwide. However, using it against systems you do not own or without written authorization is illegal. Always ensure you have proper legal authorization and documented rules of engagement before conducting any security testing.

faq.question: What is the difference between msfconsole and msfvenom?
faq.answer: msfconsole is the main Metasploit Framework interface for running exploits, managing sessions, and conducting post-exploitation activities. msfvenom is a standalone payload generator tool that creates custom executables, scripts, and shellcode for various platforms without needing the full console interface.

faq.question: Why do my exploits fail or sessions die immediately?
faq.answer: Common causes include antivirus detection, incompatible payload architecture, firewall blocking reverse connections, or crashing the target process. Solutions include using encoded payloads, matching payload architecture to the target OS, migrating to stable processes immediately, and using stageless payloads for more reliable connections.

faq.question: What are the different payload types in Metasploit?
faq.answer: Metasploit has three payload categories: Singles are self-contained payloads that execute independently. Stagers are small initial payloads that download and execute a larger stage. Stages are the full-featured payloads downloaded by stagers, providing complete functionality like Meterpreter. The naming convention follows platform/architecture/type/connection.

faq.question: How do I evade antivirus detection with Metasploit payloads?
faq.answer: Use multiple encoding iterations with the -e flag and -i parameter to obfuscate payloads. Consider using evasion modules, custom payload templates, or alternative payload types like reverse_https. For advanced evasion, you may need to create custom shellcode loaders or use third-party payload generation frameworks alongside Metasploit.

faq.question: Can this skill execute Metasploit commands directly through Claude or Claude Code?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides documentation and guidance for Metasploit Framework but does not execute commands directly. You must have Metasploit installed on your system and run commands through your terminal. The skill helps you understand proper syntax, module selection, configuration, and troubleshooting workflows.
